channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Whales Sleep Vertically: new tags reveal sperm whale bubble rests; study probes gas exchange
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Florida firefighters spotted something moving inside a storm drain during a routine call. It was 14 baby sea turtles, and they rescued every one before returning them to the ocean
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: After growing plants in space for years, NASA research could help astronauts grow medicines on demand, starting with tobacco and a harmless virus
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: In 1938, one radio broadcast made Americans think the world was ending: The incredible true story behind the 'War of the Worlds' panic
item.description: A 1938 radio drama adaptation of The War of the Worlds caused confusion among listeners. Many Americans believed they were hearing real news of a Martian invasion. This broadcast highlighted the public's trust in radio as an authoritative information source. Historians question the extent of the widespread panic initially reported. The event remains a significant lesson in media's power to influence public perception.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Thousands of idle ships trapped near the Strait of Hormuz could become floating nurseries for invasive marine species
item.description: Ships lingering in the Persian Gulf could unintentionally facilitate the spread of harmful marine invaders worldwide. Extended anchorage allows marine organisms to thrive on the hulls, creating significant biofouling concerns that threaten global maritime commerce. These invasive species can inflict extensive ecological and economic harm on coastal areas. It is imperative for nations to join forces to address biofouling issues and prevent future outbreaks of invasion.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Florida scientists used female Burmese pythons as live lures to find invasive males in Everglades
item.description: In Florida, scientists discovered that captive female pythons failed to attract wild males. Utilizing artificial intelligence, researchers sifted through millions of camera images, revealing few python sightings. Additionally, attempts to use mammalian scent lures for detecting the invasive Burmese pythons proved futile. However, there is optimism as removal strategies in the Everglades are progressing, bolstered by hunting and radio tracking initiatives aimed at controlling the invasive species population.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Scientists drill 33 feet into a vanishing glacier to recover a 2,000-year old record of...
item.description: A remarkable ice core from the Alps has revealed a comprehensive climate timeline spanning 2,000 years. It documents early instances of metal smelting, extensive forest fires, and even volcanic ash from distant eruptions. This icy archive acts as a vital record of air quality in Europe before industrial contaminants took hold. As glaciers retreat, scientists are in a race against time to safeguard these precious climate memories for future generations.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Scientists built a reusable magnetic material that removed more than 95% of microplastics from water
item.description: Innovative Australian researchers have developed a groundbreaking reusable magnetic material that effectively eliminates over ninety-five percent of microplastics from water. This remarkable technology also exhibits potential in removing harmful PFAS chemicals. The magnetic powder quickly captures tiny plastic particles and heavy metals, enabling easy recovery with magnets after use, thereby significantly cutting down on waste and operational costs. This advancement offers a promising solution for global water treatment facilities.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Florida turns restaurant waste into reefs; fish and dolphins rebound
item.description: Florida restored parts of the Gulf of Mexico by returning more than 500,000 tonnes of recycled oyster and clam shells to the sea between 2007 and 2024. The shells helped rebuild oyster reefs, improve water quality and support the return of fish, turtles and dolphins to levels not seen in over 25 years.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Pennsylvania families sell 1,700 acres for $586M; couple gets $22M for 89-acre hog farm
item.description: The AI infrastructure race has turned a quiet Pennsylvania farming community into one of the country's hottest real estate markets. As 96 families sold 1,700 acres for a combined $586 million to a Blackstone-backed data center developer, David and Marilee Kiliti received over $22 million for their 89-acre hog farm. The unexpected windfall has allowed the family to clear debts, make new investments and completely transform their future.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: FDA recalls 1.6 million dozen eggs over Salmonella risk across five US states
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Nike employees divert $2 million in Nike shoes from a Memphis warehouse in a cargo theft scheme
item.description: Federal prosecutors in the US have charged 12 people in an alleged scheme that diverted at least $2 million worth of Nike shoes from the company's Memphis logistics centre. According to the indictment, unauthorised UPS shipping labels were used to redirect thousands of cartons to resellers across the country. The defendants deny no charges yet, and the allegations will be tested in court.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Scientists are dropping thousands of fish from airplanes into remote mountain lakes; here's why the unusual conservation strategy has worked for decades
item.description: Utah's Division of Wildlife Resources stocks remote mountain lakes by dropping fish from airplanes. This method efficiently reaches inaccessible waters, maintaining fish populations for anglers. Tiny trout and other species are released, with survival rates exceeding ninety-five percent. Biologists carefully select species, often using sterile fish to control numbers and protect native ecosystems. This practice balances recreation with conservation in challenging mountainous terrain.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: California to spend $36.4 mn repairing historic Ebbetts Pass where highway climbs 8000 feet
item.description: Caltrans will begin a $36.4 million project on August 3 to repair 31.7 miles of California's Highway 4, including the historic Ebbetts Pass in the Sierra Nevada. The four-year project will improve road surfaces, drainage and safety features while causing temporary lane closures and occasional full highway closures until 2030.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Fired for $1.95 snack, Ford worker gets $33K back pay after showing receipt
item.description: A Ford employee faced termination after an alleged cookie theft from a self-checkout kiosk. Bank records later proved the $1.95 snack had been paid for by the diabetic worker. Ford acknowledged the error and offered the employee back wages and his old job. However, the former electrician declined the offer and is now preparing to sue the automaker. This incident highlights concerns about workplace technology and employee investigation protocols.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: She inherited her childhood home, then a $228,000 water bill appeared
item.description: A woman in Baltimore, US, says she was shocked to discover a $228,000 unpaid water bill attached to her late father's home after inheriting the property. The debt has placed a lien on the house, preventing its sale. The dispute centres on whether the homeowner or the community's homeowners association should be responsible for the outstanding amount.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Chile community beats drought by restoring forests, soil and watershed
item.description: A rural community on Chile's Chiloe Island has restored its water supply after years of relying on emergency tanker trucks. By protecting forests, restoring its watershed, building a gravity-fed water network and involving local residents in maintenance, the community has maintained a reliable system for nearly seven years. The project is now being presented as a model for other rural areas facing water shortages.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Orcas filmed tearing apart giant Sunfish with new hunting technique
item.description: Researchers have documented a previously unknown hunting behaviour in orcas in the Gulf of California. The animals were filmed working together to hold and ram giant sharp-tail sunfish until the carcass broke into thousands of pieces. Scientists believe the behaviour may help younger orcas feed more easily or could simply be play.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: A backyard chicken owner thought hens were eating the missing eggs, but later found...
item.description: Chicken keepers report sightings of snakes feasting on their eggs within nesting boxes. These serpents are drawn to the warmth of coops and the presence of stored chicken feed. Experts advocate using humane deterrents instead of harsh chemicals for effective long-term management. Regularly collecting eggs and securing feed bins can effectively keep these unwanted guests at bay. Enhancing coops can safeguard eggs while allowing snakes to maintain the ecosystem's balance.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Spain total solar eclipse 12 August 2026: Why thousands of eclipse chasers could miss the total solar eclipse
item.description: Spain anticipates thousands for a significant 2026 solar eclipse event. The eclipse's late timing near sunset presents unique viewing challenges. Obstacles like trees and hills could block the low-hanging Sun. Careful site selection and elevation are crucial for clear views. This event begins a rare three-year series of eclipses in Spain.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
